title: React Server Components
summary: RSC architecture, server vs client components, server actions, streaming, and the mental model shift from traditional React.

questions.prompt: How do React Server Components differ from traditional React components (Client Components) in terms of where they run?
choices: Both run in the browser — RSC just runs first
RSC runs on the server at request time, never ships to the browser. Client components run in the browser
RSC runs in a web worker
RSC runs on the server only during build time
questions.explanation: Server components execute on the server — they can access databases, file systems, and secrets directly. Their output (React element tree) is sent to the browser, but the component code itself never runs in the browser. This means their code isn't in the JavaScript bundle.

questions.prompt: What does `'use client'` at the top of a file declare?
choices: This component and everything imported below it forms a client boundary — runs in the browser with access to React hooks and browser APIs
This file is excluded from server-side rendering
Only functions in this file are client-side
questions.explanation: 'use client' is a directive that marks a boundary. The component and all its imports become client components — they're included in the JavaScript bundle and run in the browser. This is how you opt into useState, event handlers, and browser APIs.

questions.prompt: A Server Component can directly:
choices: Call useState and add event handlers
Query a database, read environment variables, and access the file system without any API layer
questions.explanation: Server components run in a Node.js (or edge) environment — they have full access to database drivers, file system APIs, and process.env secrets. This eliminates the need for API routes to proxy server-only operations. The data access code never reaches the browser.

questions.prompt: What does Suspense do in the context of React Server Component streaming?
choices: Prevents the page from loading until all data is ready
Acts as a boundary — static content streams immediately, content inside Suspense streams when its async data resolves
questions.explanation: With streaming, the server sends HTML progressively. Suspense boundaries act as 'wait here' markers. Static parts of the page stream immediately. Parts wrapped in Suspense stream their fallback HTML first, then replace it with real content when the async component's data is ready.

questions.prompt: Server Components contribute to the client-side JavaScript bundle:
choices: Zero — server component code runs on the server and is never included in the browser bundle
questions.explanation: Server components are fundamentally different from lazy-loaded client components. Lazy loading defers when a component's code is downloaded. RSC means the component's code is never downloaded at all — it runs only on the server. This can dramatically reduce bundle size.

questions.prompt: Can you pass a function as a prop from a Server Component to a Client Component?
choices: No — props crossing the server→client boundary must be JSON-serialisable; functions are not serialisable
questions.explanation: The server must serialise props to send them to the client (as part of the RSC payload). Functions cannot be serialised to JSON and sent over a network. Client components that need callbacks must define those callbacks inside their own client boundary (use client).

questions.prompt: Server Actions (marked 'use server') differ from API routes by:
choices: They are TypeScript functions called directly from components — no manual fetch, no route file, no request/response parsing
questions.explanation: Server Actions let you write a function with 'use server', call it from a form or client event, and Next.js/React handles the network call automatically. No `fetch('/api/...')`, no JSON stringify, no response parsing. The function runs on the server with direct DB access.

questions.prompt: What happens if you try to use `useState` inside a Server Component?
choices: React throws an error — useState requires a client environment (re-rendering, event loop). Server components run once and return HTML
It works but triggers no re-renders
questions.explanation: Server components execute once on the server — there's no user interaction cycle, no DOM, no event loop for re-renders. Hooks like useState, useEffect, and useCallback require the client runtime. React throws an error if you use them in a server component.

questions.prompt: In this async server component, which is better and why?
choices: B is better — both requests run simultaneously; total time = max(getUser, getOrders) not sum
A is faster when one request depends on the other
questions.explanation: In Option A: if getUser takes 200ms and getOrders takes 300ms, total = 500ms. In Option B: both start simultaneously, total = 300ms (the slower one). Always use Promise.all for independent fetches. Option A (waterfall) is only correct when the second fetch needs the first result.

questions.prompt: How does Streaming improve the user experience compared to waiting for all server data?
choices: Static parts of the page appear immediately; slow data sections show skeletons and replace themselves as data arrives — users see progress instead of a blank page
questions.explanation: Without streaming: server renders everything, sends one HTML blob. With streaming: server sends partial HTML as each part becomes ready. A user sees the header and navigation in 50ms, product list in 200ms, recommendations in 800ms — versus a blank page until 800ms.

questions.prompt: Can a Client Component render a Server Component as children?
choices: No — client components cannot render server components
Yes — by receiving server component output as the `children` prop (rendered by a parent server component)
questions.explanation: The pattern: a Server Component renders `<ClientShell><ServerContent /></ClientShell>`. ServerContent is rendered on the server and passed as children to ClientShell (a client component). ClientShell renders it as JSX. This avoids breaking the client boundary while still having interactivity around server-rendered content.

questions.prompt: In Next.js App Router, `revalidatePath('/products')` inside a Server Action:
choices: Invalidates the cached data for the /products route — the next request re-fetches and re-renders with fresh data
questions.explanation: Next.js caches server component outputs. After a mutation (Server Action), you need to invalidate the cache so users see updated data. `revalidatePath('/products')` marks that path's cache as stale — the next request triggers a fresh render. Alternatively, `revalidateTag` for granular cache control.

questions.prompt: Which belong in a Server Component?
choices: Database query to fetch page data
Click event handler for a button
Rendering a complex static layout with no interactivity
questions.explanation: DB queries (A), secret env vars (C), and static layouts (D) all belong in server components — they have no need for the browser runtime. Click handlers (B) require event listeners which are browser-only — they belong in client components ('use client').

questions.prompt: What problem does the 'use server' directive on a Server Action solve vs a traditional API route?
choices: Eliminates the separate API file, fetch call, JSON parsing, and type duplication — the action is a typed TypeScript function callable directly
It runs faster due to built-in caching
questions.explanation: Traditional: create app/api/orders/route.ts, write fetch('/api/orders', {...}), parse JSON response, handle errors, maintain types on both sides. Server Action: write one async function, call it directly from a form or client component. Next.js handles the network transport. One file, one type definition.

questions.prompt: What type of rendering model does RSC replace in Next.js's App Router (vs the old Pages Router)?
choices: getServerSideProps / getStaticProps — those were HOF patterns to fetch data for a page; RSC async components fetch data directly
questions.explanation: Next.js Pages Router used `getServerSideProps` to fetch data and pass it as props to the page component. App Router RSC replaces this — the component itself is async and fetches data inline at the top. No HOF wrapper, no special return format, just a regular async function.

questions.prompt: A large markdown blog post component that only reads from the filesystem has no user interactivity. Making it a Server Component:
choices: Removes ALL its JavaScript from the client bundle — markdown parser, syntax highlighter, etc. only run on the server. Zero JS shipped for this component
Prevents it from rendering on the client
questions.explanation: This is RSC's compelling use case. A markdown renderer might import `remark`, `remark-gfm`, a syntax highlighter — 50-100KB of libraries. As a server component, none of that reaches the browser. The output is plain HTML. 100KB removed from the client bundle.

questions.prompt: What does `loading.tsx` do in Next.js App Router?
choices: Shows a loading bar at the top of the page
Automatically acts as a Suspense fallback for the route's page.tsx — shown while streaming server data
questions.explanation: Next.js App Router automatically wraps the segment's page.tsx in a Suspense boundary when a `loading.tsx` file is present. It shows immediately (before async data resolves) alongside any already-streamed content. No manual `<Suspense>` wrapper needed.

questions.prompt: Which are valid ways to fetch data in React Server Components?
choices: fetch() with Next.js cache options: `fetch(url, { next: { revalidate: 60 } })`
questions.explanation: Server components can use any server-side data access: ORM (A), extended fetch (B), parallel Promise.all (D). useEffect (C) doesn't exist in server components — it requires the client React runtime and re-renders.

questions.prompt: An image component in a Server Component imports a 400KB image processing library. If this stays a Server Component:
choices: The library downloads on every request
The library runs on the server — 400KB never included in the client JS bundle — user downloads only the resulting HTML/img tags
The library is cached in the browser
It causes an error — server components can't use libraries
questions.explanation: Server component imports run on the server. The image processing library never reaches the browser. The component produces HTML (an `` tag) which is streamed to the client. This is one of RSC's key benefits — heavy processing stays on the server.

questions.prompt: The biggest mental model shift when moving from CSR React to React Server Components is:
choices: Thinking about WHERE code runs — not all components are equal; async server components replace useEffect+useState for data fetching; 'use client' is opt-in interactivity
questions.explanation: The fundamental shift: in CSR, all components are client-side by default. In RSC, components are server-side by default — you opt into the browser environment with 'use client'. Data fetching moves out of useEffect into async component bodies. Interactive islands use 'use client' at their boundaries.

flashcards.back: Server: runs on server, async/await, DB/FS access, zero JS to browser. Client ('use client'): runs in browser, useState/useEffect, event handlers, browser APIs.
A boundary. The file + everything imported under it = client component (ships to browser). Place it as deep as possible to maximise server component territory.
Server component code NEVER in JS bundle. Heavy libraries (markdown parsers, image processors) run server-side only. Dramatic bundle size reduction.
Async function running on server, called from client. Direct DB access. No API route needed. Works as HTML form action for progressive enhancement.
Static parts stream immediately. Suspense-wrapped async parts show skeleton until data resolves. User sees content progressively — not blank then all-at-once.
Must be JSON-serialisable. No functions, class instances, or circular refs. Strings, numbers, plain objects, arrays: OK.
`await Promise.all([getUser(), getOrders()])` — run simultaneously. Avoid sequential awaits for independent queries (waterfall = sum of times vs max of times).
Called in Server Action after mutation. Invalidates Next.js cache for a path or tag. Next request fetches fresh data. Replaces client cache invalidation logic.

project.brief: Design a blog platform using React Server Components (Next.js 13+ App Router). It has: a homepage listing recent posts, individual post pages with markdown content, a comment system with real-time add/delete, and an admin section to publish/unpublish posts. Design: 1) Which components are Server Components and which need 'use client'? 2) How is post data fetched (no API routes)? 3) How does the comment form work as a Server Action? 4) How does the admin publish action invalidate cached post pages? 5) Why is the markdown renderer a perfect Server Component?

checklist.text: Homepage and BlogPost page: Server Components — async fetch from DB, zero client JS for content
CommentForm: Client Component ('use client') for optimistic UI. Or Server Action form for progressive enhancement
addComment Server Action: writes to DB, calls revalidatePath('/posts/[id]') to refresh comments
publishPost Server Action: DB update + revalidatePath('/') and revalidatePath('/posts/[id]')
Markdown renderer: imports remark/remark-gfm (heavy libs), runs on server only — zero markdown JS in client bundle

hints: Post page: async function PostPage({ params }) { const post = await db.post.findUnique({where:{id:params.id}}); return <Markdown source={post.content} />; }
CommentForm with Server Action: <form action={addComment}><input name='text' /><button>Add</button></form> works without JavaScript.
revalidatePath after addComment ensures the cached post page shows new comments on next view.
Markdown renderer: import remark from 'remark' etc. — in Server Component only. Browser never downloads these packages.
